problem_solved: The proof establishes the claimed result under the stated/explicit interpretation that there is exactly one vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\): for any finite weighted tree \(T\) whose associated integral symmetric form is positive definite and has a unique such “bad” vertex, \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex of \(L(T)\). More specifically, if \(T\) has a weight-one vertex, that vertex is irreducible; otherwise the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

minor_gaps_text:
- EXPOSITION ONLY — The proof interprets “a single vertex” as “a unique vertex.” This is consistent with the wording if “single” is read literally, but the theorem statement should preferably say “exactly one” or “unique” to avoid ambiguity.
- EXPOSITION ONLY — In the induction step of the rooted branch lemma, the proof should explicitly say that each component matrix \(A_i\) is positive definite because it is a principal submatrix of the positive-definite matrix \(A\). This is routine and does not affect correctness.

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
No correctness-relevant patch is required.

Optional insertions for clarity:
1. “Since \(A_i\) is a principal submatrix of \(A\), it is positive definite, so the induction hypothesis applies to \(C_i\).”
2. Replace “there exists a single vertex” by “there exists a unique vertex” if uniqueness is intended.
problem_solved: Assuming “a single vertex” means exactly one vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\), the proof establishes the stated theorem: every positive-definite integer-weighted finite tree with a unique bad vertex contains an irreducible vertex. More specifically, any weight-one vertex is irreducible; if no weight-one vertex exists, then the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

The proof establishes the claimed theorem under the interpretation that there is exactly one bad vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\): for any finite integer-weighted tree whose associated bilinear form is positive definite and whose bad vertex is unique, \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More specifically, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if no vertex has weight one, then the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

Assuming “there exists a single vertex” means “there exists exactly one bad vertex,” the proof establishes the stated theorem: for a finite weighted tree whose associated integral bilinear form is positive definite and which has exactly one vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\), the tree contains an irreducible vertex. More specifically, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if there is no weight-one vertex, then the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.
